text.title: Risk Register — Six-Step Process
text.summary: How to build and maintain a risk register: identify risks by category, analyze probability and impact, score on a 5x5 matrix, plan responses (threat and opportunity strategies), assign owners, and run weekly reviews.

section.title: Step 1: Identify Risks

rule.statement: Identify both threats (negative risks) and opportunities (positive risks) across all four categories: Technical, External, Organizational, and Project Management.
rule.rationale: Registers that capture only threats miss the opportunity to exploit or enhance favorable events. Systematic category coverage prevents blind spots from informal brainstorming.

section.p: Identification techniques: team brainstorming, expert interviews, checklist review, historical project analysis, SWOT analysis.

section.title: Step 2: Analyze Probability and Impact
section.p: Probability scale (1=Very Low to 5=Very High): VL < 10%, L 10-30%, M 30-50%, H 50-70%, VH > 70%.
Impact scale (1=Very Low to 5=Very High): VL < 5% budget/schedule, L 5-10%, M 10-20%, H 20-40%, VH > 40% or project failure.

rule.statement: Compute Risk Score = Probability x Impact (1–25); prioritize as High (15–25), Medium (8–14), Low (1–7). Require per-axis rationale for every score to prevent clustering at Medium-Medium.
rule.rationale: Without rationale, analysts default to Medium-Medium, compressing information and hiding true high-priority risks. Per-axis justification forces calibration and distributes scores.

rule.statement: Pair every risk with a measurable trigger condition; without a trigger, the team cannot detect when the risk is activating.
rule.rationale: Trigger-less risks stay academic. A trigger converts risk monitoring from a scheduled exercise to an event-driven alert.

section.title: Step 4: Plan Responses
section.p: For threats: Avoid (eliminate), Transfer (insurance/outsource), Mitigate (reduce P or I), Accept (contingency).
For opportunities: Exploit (ensure it occurs), Share (partner), Enhance (increase probability), Accept (take benefit if it occurs).

rule.statement: Every risk with score 8 or above must have a named strategy and a specific response action; "Accept" without a contingency is not a valid response for high-score risks.
rule.rationale: The all-accept antipattern gives false comfort. High-score risks without funded contingency will hit the budget as unplanned crisis costs.

rule.statement: Assign exactly one named owner per risk; "the team" is not an owner.
rule.rationale: Shared ownership is no ownership. One owner is accountable for monitoring triggers, executing response if needed, and updating status.

section.title: Step 6: Monitor and Update Weekly

rule.statement: Review risks weekly; add new entries, update scores as situation changes, and close risks with explicit outcome (materialized / passed / merged). Never delete a row.
rule.rationale: Deletion destroys audit history and contingency calculation integrity. Closure with outcome preserves the record for lessons-learned and future project calibration.

section.p: Weekly review agenda: new risks? scores changed? triggers activated? responses in progress? risks to close? top-5 still accurate?
section.reference: Script that flags stale, high-priority, and no-owner risks from a CSV register.
